docs.title: Versioning Scheme
docs.text:
We use Semantic Versioning: - MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH-status - MAJOR: Breaking changes - MINOR: New features, no breaking changes - PATCH: Bug fixes only - status: alpha, beta, rc (release candidate), or omitted for stable

Pre-1.0 releases are not API-stable.

docs.title: Contributing to the Roadmap
docs.text:
Want to influence priorities? Open an issue or PR with: - Use case: who would use this and why - Effort estimate: rough idea of complexity - Dependencies: what it needs

Or just start building it. Working code beats arguing.

docs.title: The Science Behind OpenDNA
docs.text: This document explains what each algorithm in OpenDNA actually does, the science behind it, and what the results mean.

docs.title: Table of Contents
docs.text:
1. Protein Folding (ESMFold)
  2. Inverse Folding (ESM-IF1)
  3. Sequence Properties
  4. Lipinski's Rule of Five
  5. Hydropathy Analysis
  6. Intrinsic Disorder
  7. Secondary Structure
  8. Ramachandran Plot
  9. Transmembrane Prediction
  10. Signal Peptide Detection
  11. Aggregation Prediction
  12. Post-Translational Modifications
  13. Mutation Effects (ΔΔG)
  14. Bond Detection
  15. Iterative Design

docs.title: 1. Protein Folding (ESMFold)
docs.text:
What it does: Takes an amino acid sequence and predicts the 3D coordinates of every atom in the protein.

The model: ESMFold v1 by Meta AI (Lin et al., 2022). A transformer-based language model trained on 65 million protein sequences. Unlike AlphaFold2, ESMFold doesn't need a multiple sequence alignment — it works on a single sequence directly, which makes it ~60x faster.

How it works (in 3 sentences): 1. The sequence is fed through ESM-2, a protein language model that learns evolutionary patterns from raw sequences. 2. The internal embeddings capture which residues are likely to be near each other in 3D. 3. A folding head decodes those embeddings into atomic coordinates.

Output: - 3D coordinates in PDB format - pLDDT confidence score (per-residue, 0-100): how sure the model is about each position - 90+: very high confidence - 70-90: confident - 50-70: low confidence (often disordered) - <50: very low confidence (random)

Limitations: - Less accurate than full AlphaFold2 with MSA, especially for novel folds - Hard time with multimers (use AlphaFold-Multimer instead) - Can hallucinate confident-looking structures for sequences with no evolutionary history

Why it matters: This used to require a HPC cluster and weeks of compute. Now it runs on a gaming laptop in minutes.

docs.title: 2. Inverse Folding (ESM-IF1)
docs.text:
What it does: Takes a 3D backbone structure and generates amino acid sequences that should fold into that shape.

The model: ESM-IF1 by Meta AI (Hsu et al., 2022). A geometric vector perceptron transformer trained on 12 million predicted structures.

How it works: 1. Read the backbone N, Cα, C, O atom coordinates 2. The encoder builds a representation of the 3D environment around each residue 3. The decoder samples a new amino acid for each position, conditioned on the geometry 4. Higher temperature = more diverse sequences (less native-like)

Output: - Multiple alternative sequences - Recovery percentage: how many residues match the original sequence - High recovery (50%+) = conservative redesign - Low recovery (10-30%) = aggressive redesign of the same fold - A score (lower is better — derived from negative log-likelihood)

Use cases: - Stabilize a protein without changing its function - Diversify a binder to find better variants - Remove problematic regions (e.g. aggregation-prone, immunogenic) - Make a protein easier to manufacture

The trick of "design then verify": Generated sequences should fold into the original backbone. To verify, fold them with ESMFold and compare. This is exactly what OpenDNA's iterative design loop does.

docs.title: 3. Sequence Properties
docs.text:
Molecular Weight (MW): Sum of amino acid masses minus water for each peptide bond. Reported in Daltons. A 100 aa protein is ~11,000 Da (≈11 kDa).

Isoelectric Point (pI): The pH at which the protein has zero net charge. Calculated by bisection on the net charge equation, summing contributions from each ionizable group: 

charge(pH) = Σ positive groups (1 / (1 + 10^(pH - pKa)))
           - Σ negative groups (1 / (1 + 10^(pKa - pH)))
- pI < 5: very acidic (likely DNA-binding or calcium-binding) - pI 5-7: typical cytoplasmic - pI 7-9: typical secreted - pI > 9: very basic (histones, ribosomal proteins)

GRAVY (Grand Average of Hydropathy): Average Kyte-Doolittle hydropathy across the sequence. - GRAVY > 0: hydrophobic (membrane proteins, oils) - GRAVY < 0: hydrophilic (soluble, cytoplasmic)

Aromaticity: Fraction of F+W+Y residues. Affects UV absorbance and folding.

Instability Index (Guruprasad et al. 1990): Sum of dipeptide instability values. - Score < 40: stable in vivo - Score > 40: unstable, may degrade quickly

Aliphatic Index (Ikai 1980): Volume occupied by aliphatic side chains (A, V, I, L). Higher = more thermostable.

N-terminal Half-life: Predicted half-life in mammalian cells based on the N-terminal amino acid (the "N-end rule" - Bachmair et al. 1986). Methionine and valine give long half-lives; arginine, lysine, glutamate give short ones.

Extinction Coefficient (Pace et al.): Predicts UV absorbance at 280 nm: 

ε(280nm) = (Trp × 5500) + (Tyr × 1490) + (Cys/2 × 125)
Used to determine protein concentration spectrophotometrically.

docs.title: 4. Lipinski's Rule of Five
docs.text:
What it is: A quick drug-likeness check developed by Christopher Lipinski at Pfizer in 1997 for predicting oral bioavailability of small molecules.

The rules: 1. Molecular weight ≤ 500 Da 2. Hydrogen bond donors ≤ 5 3. Hydrogen bond acceptors ≤ 10 4. LogP (lipophilicity) ≤ 5

A drug should violate at most one rule to be likely orally available.

Why we apply it to peptides: Most peptides will fail RO5 (they're too big), but the analysis still gives useful info on whether a small peptide could be a drug.

Caveats: - Originally designed for small molecules, not proteins - Doesn't predict everything (membrane permeability, toxicity, etc.) - Many useful drugs violate it (Lipinski himself estimates 40% of drugs fail)

docs.title: 5. Hydropathy Analysis
docs.text:
Algorithm: Kyte-Doolittle (1982). Each amino acid has a hydropathy value:

Most hydrophobic Most hydrophilic I (+4.5), V (+4.2), L (+3.8) R (-4.5), K (-3.9), D/N/Q/E (-3.5) F (+2.8), C (+2.5), M (+1.9) H (-3.2), Y (-1.3), P (-1.6)

Output: A sliding window average across the sequence (window=9 by default).

What to look for: - Peaks above +1.6 over ~19 residues = transmembrane helices - Long stretches above 0 = hydrophobic domains (membrane interaction, oily core) - Long stretches below 0 = hydrophilic surface or disordered regions - Sharp transitions = signal peptides, hinge regions

docs.title: 6. Intrinsic Disorder
docs.text:
What it is: Many proteins (or regions of proteins) don't fold into a fixed structure. They remain flexible and dynamic in solution. These "intrinsically disordered proteins/regions" (IDPs/IDRs) account for 30-50% of the human proteome.

Why it matters: - Disordered regions are common in transcription factors, signaling proteins, and scaffolds - Drug targets often have disordered regions that are hard to crystallize - p53 (the famous tumor suppressor) is mostly disordered - Disorder is biologically functional, not just noise

Algorithm: IUPred-like sliding window on a per-residue propensity scale (Linding et al. 2003 simplified).

Output: - Per-residue probability (0-1) - Identified disordered regions (≥5 consecutive residues with score ≥0.5) - Overall disorder percentage

Try this: Fetch p53 (P04637), run analysis. You'll see ~70% disorder.

docs.title: 7. Secondary Structure
docs.text:
What it is: Beyond the linear amino acid sequence, the local 3D shape forms recurring patterns: - α-helix (H): Right-handed coil, 3.6 residues per turn, stabilized by H-bonds - β-strand (E): Extended chain, often paired with other strands to form sheets - Coil (C): Everything else (loops, turns, disordered)

Algorithm: OpenDNA uses a simplified DSSP-like assignment based on phi/psi dihedral angles: - Helix region: -160 < φ < -40 AND -70 < ψ < -10 - Strand region: -180 < φ < -40 AND 90 < ψ < 180 - Otherwise: coil

Output: Per-residue assignment string like HHHHCCCEEECC... and percentages.

Real DSSP (Kabsch & Sander 1983) uses backbone hydrogen bond patterns and is more accurate. OpenDNA's version is fast and good enough for visualization.

docs.title: 8. Ramachandran Plot
docs.text:
What it is: A scatter plot of phi (φ) vs psi (ψ) backbone dihedral angles for every residue. Named after G.N. Ramachandran (1963).

Why it's useful: - Most residues cluster in two regions: alpha-helical (-60, -45) and beta-sheet (-120, +120) - Outliers indicate strain, errors, or unusual conformations (often glycine or proline) - A "good" structure has >90% of residues in favored regions

How OpenDNA computes it: - For each residue, compute φ from the previous C-N-CA-C atoms - Compute ψ from the current N-CA-C-N atoms - Plot all pairs

Visual interpretation: - Top-left cluster (~-60, -45) = right-handed α-helix - Top-right cluster (~-120, +120) = β-sheet - Bottom-left = left-handed α-helix (rare, mostly glycines) - Sparse points everywhere = bad structure or many disordered residues

docs.title: 9. Transmembrane Prediction
docs.text:
What it is: Identifies regions that span a lipid membrane. Membrane proteins have hydrophobic helices long enough to cross the bilayer (~20 Å, typically 17-25 amino acids).

Algorithm: Sliding-window hydropathy. A region of ≥17 consecutive residues with average hydropathy >1.4 is flagged as a transmembrane (TM) helix.

Output: - Per-residue scores - TM region boundaries - Total helix count - "Is membrane protein" flag

Real TMHMM (Krogh et al.) uses a hidden Markov model that's more accurate for distinguishing TM helices from signal peptides and predicting topology (which side is in/out). Our version is the simple version.

Test it: Try the COVID spike protein (spike_covid or P0DTC2) — it has 1 TM helix.

docs.title: 10. Signal Peptide Detection
docs.text:
What they are: Short N-terminal sequences (15-30 aa) that direct proteins to be secreted from the cell. Cleaved off by signal peptidase during translocation.

Structure of a signal peptide: - n-region (positively charged, 1-5 aa) - h-region (hydrophobic core, 7-15 aa) - c-region (polar, 3-7 aa, ends with cleavage motif)

Algorithm (heuristic): Score the first 30 residues for these three regions. If all three look right, predict a signal peptide and find the cleavage site (usually after a small residue like A, G, S, C, T at position -1, with another small residue at position -3).

Real SignalP uses neural networks trained on thousands of validated signal peptides. Our heuristic catches the obvious cases.

Use case: Knowing if your designed protein will be secreted vs cytoplasmic.

docs.title: 11. Aggregation Prediction
docs.text:
What it is: Many designed proteins form insoluble aggregates instead of folding properly. This is one of the biggest problems in protein engineering.

Algorithm: TANGO/Aggrescan-like. Each residue has an aggregation propensity: - High (aggregating): I, F, V, L, Y, W - Low (gatekeeper): P, K, R, D, E (charged residues block aggregation)

A region of ≥5 residues with average propensity >1.0 is flagged as an "aggregation-prone region" (APR).

Output: - Per-residue scores - Identified APRs with sequences - Overall aggregation score - Risk level (low / medium / high)

Real TANGO and Aggrescan are slightly different approaches but the principle is the same. Our version is good enough for screening.

Engineering tip: If your protein has high aggregation risk, mutate hydrophobic residues in the APR to charged residues (D, E, K, R) — they act as "gatekeepers."

docs.title: Phosphorylation
docs.text:
Adding a phosphate group to S, T, or Y residues. Regulates ~30% of all proteins.

OpenDNA detects: Sequence motifs matching consensus sites for major kinases: - PKA (Protein Kinase A): R-R/K-X-S/T - PKC (Protein Kinase C): S/T-X-R/K - CK2 (Casein Kinase 2): S/T-X-X-D/E - CDK (Cyclin-Dependent Kinase): S/T-P-X-K/R - GSK3 (Glycogen Synthase Kinase 3): S/T-X-X-X-S/T - MAPK: P-X-S/T-P

Real prediction: NetPhos uses ML models. Motif-based gives many false positives but catches the obvious ones.

docs.title: Glycosylation
docs.text:
Adding sugar chains to specific residues. Very common in secreted and membrane proteins.

  • N-linked: N-X-S/T sequon (X is any residue except proline). Most common.
  • O-linked: S/T residues, often in mucin-like (Pro/Ser/Thr-rich) regions.

Why it matters: Glycosylation affects folding, stability, immunogenicity, and half-life. Missing or wrong glycosylation can ruin a therapeutic.

docs.title: 13. Mutation Effects (ΔΔG)
docs.text:
What it is: Predict whether a mutation will stabilize or destabilize the protein.

Convention: - ΔΔG = ΔG(mutant) - ΔG(wild-type), in kcal/mol - Negative ΔΔG = destabilizing (mutation makes protein less stable) - Positive ΔΔG = stabilizing - |ΔΔG| < 0.5: probably neutral - |ΔΔG| > 1.5: significant change

OpenDNA's algorithm: A heuristic based on: 1. Per-residue stability propensity differences 2. Penalty for radical changes (e.g. hydrophobic → charged) 3. Penalty for changes in special residues (P, G, C)

Real prediction: FoldX, Rosetta Cartesian_ddG, or trained ML models like ProTSTaB. These do explicit physics or learn from thermal melting datasets.

Use case: Quick screen of which mutations to try in the lab.

docs.title: 14. Bond Detection
docs.text: OpenDNA scans the structure for:

docs.title: Hydrogen Bonds
docs.text: N-H...O or O-H...N pairs within 2.5-3.5 Å between non-adjacent residues. Stabilize secondary structure.

docs.title: Salt Bridges
docs.text:
Charged side chain pairs within 4 Å: - Positive: Lys (NZ), Arg (NH1, NH2, NE), His (ND1, NE2) - Negative: Asp (OD1, OD2), Glu (OE1, OE2)

Salt bridges are strong electrostatic interactions, especially valuable for thermostability.

docs.title: Disulfide Bonds
docs.text: Cys SG-SG pairs within 3 Å. Covalent bonds that staple two cysteines together. Very common in secreted proteins (their extra stability helps survive outside the cell). Insulin has 3 disulfides.

docs.title: 15. Iterative Design
docs.text:
The killer feature. Combines folding + design in a loop.

Algorithm: 1. Fold the input sequence with ESMFold → get backbone + score 2. For N rounds: a. Use ESM-IF1 to generate K candidate sequences for the current backbone b. Score each candidate (sequence-based scoring is fast) c. Find the highest-scoring candidate d. If it's better than current best, fold it with ESMFold to confirm e. Update current best if confirmed 3. Return the optimized protein

Why it works: Each round explores the local sequence space around a stable backbone. Over multiple rounds, it climbs toward higher-scoring proteins while maintaining the structural fold.

Comparable commercial features: - Schrödinger BioLuminate Mutation/Stability calculations + Manual loop - Rosetta Cartesian_ddG + scripts - Schrödinger IFD-MD Induced Fit Docking + MD refinement loop

Cost in commercial software: $50,000-$200,000/year per seat. Cost in OpenDNA: $0. Runs on your laptop.

docs.title: References
docs.text:
• ESMFold: Lin, Z. et al. (2023). "Evolutionary-scale prediction of atomic-level protein structure." Science 379, 1123-1130.
  • ESM-IF1: Hsu, C. et al. (2022). "Learning inverse folding from millions of predicted structures." ICML.
  • Kyte-Doolittle: Kyte, J. & Doolittle, R.F. (1982). J. Mol. Biol. 157, 105-132.
  • DSSP: Kabsch, W. & Sander, C. (1983). Biopolymers 22, 2577-2637.
  • Ramachandran: Ramachandran, G.N. et al. (1963). J. Mol. Biol. 7, 95-99.
  • TMHMM: Krogh, A. et al. (2001). J. Mol. Biol. 305, 567-580.
  • SignalP: Almagro Armenteros, J.J. et al. (2019). Nat. Biotechnol. 37, 420-423.
  • TANGO: Fernandez-Escamilla, A.M. et al. (2004). Nat. Biotechnol. 22, 1302-1306.
  • IUPred: Dosztányi, Z. et al. (2005). J. Mol. Biol. 347, 827-839.
  • Lipinski: Lipinski, C.A. et al. (1997). Adv. Drug Deliv. Rev. 23, 3-25.
  • Instability Index: Guruprasad, K. et al. (1990). Protein Eng. 4, 155-161.
  • N-end rule: Bachmair, A. et al. (1986). Science 234, 179-186.

docs.title: OpenDNA Troubleshooting
docs.text: When things break, look here first.

docs.title: "ERROR: bind on address ('0.0.0.0', 8000): permission denied"
docs.text:
Cause: Port 8000 is reserved or in use on your system (common on Windows).

Fix: Use a different port: 

python -c "from opendna.api.server import start_server; start_server(port=8765)"

You'll need to also update the UI to point to that port. Check ui/src/api/client.ts line 3: 

const API_BASE = "http://localhost:8765";

docs.title: "ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'opendna'"
docs.text:
Cause: You haven't installed the Python package.

Fix: 

cd /path/to/opendna
pip install -e ".[dev]"

docs.title: "ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'fastapi'"
docs.text:
Cause: Missing dependencies.

Fix: 

pip install -e ".[dev]"

If that fails, try: 

pip install fastapi uvicorn[standard] sqlalchemy typer rich pydantic httpx pyyaml sse-starlette psutil torch transformers fair-esm biopython biotite torch-geometric

docs.title: "Torch not compiled with CUDA enabled"
docs.text:
Cause: You have an NVIDIA GPU but installed CPU-only PyTorch. The hardware detector says "use CUDA" but PyTorch can't.

Fix option 1 - Install CUDA torch: 

pip uninstall torch -y
pip install torch --index-url https://download.pytorch.org/whl/cu124

Fix option 2 - Force CPU mode in your fold call: The latest version of hardware/detect.py already verifies torch supports the backend before recommending it. If you still hit this, restart the API server.

docs.title: Folding hangs at "Loading weights"
docs.text:
Cause: Model is downloading from HuggingFace Hub. ESMFold is ~8 GB.

Fix: Wait. Watch the API terminal for download progress. If it stays at 0% for >5 minutes, check your internet connection.

docs.title: Folding completes but viewer shows empty
docs.text:
Cause: UI didn't receive the result, or PDB couldn't be parsed.

Fix: 1. Open browser DevTools (F12) → Console tab 2. Look for errors during the fold completion 3. Check API terminal for any errors 4. Try a shorter sequence to test

docs.title: "RuntimeError: CUDA out of memory"
docs.text:
Cause: Your GPU doesn't have enough VRAM for this protein.

Fix: - Try a shorter sequence - Close other GPU-using applications - Switch to CPU mode (slower but works)

docs.title: Folding takes forever on CPU
docs.text:
That's expected. ESMFold on CPU is slow: - 30 residues: ~1 minute - 100 residues: ~5-10 minutes - 200 residues: ~30 minutes - 500+ residues: not recommended

Speedup options: - Get an NVIDIA GPU - Get a Mac with Apple Silicon (MPS support) - Use a cloud GPU (Lambda Labs, RunPod, etc.) — manual setup for now

docs.title: Blank white screen
docs.text:
Cause: UI dev server isn't running, or there's a build error.

Fix: 1. Make sure npm run dev is running in ui/ 2. Check the Vite terminal for errors 3. Open browser DevTools (F12) → Console for runtime errors 4. Try npm install in ui/ to reinstall dependencies

docs.title: "Failed to fetch" errors
docs.text:
Cause: API server isn't running, or wrong port.

Fix: 1. Make sure the API server is running on port 8765 2. Check the URL in ui/src/api/client.ts 3. Try curl http://localhost:8765/health to verify the API works

docs.title: 3D viewer is blank/black
docs.text:
Cause: Molstar failed to load, or no PDB data.

Fix: 1. Open DevTools console for Molstar errors 2. Make sure you've actually folded a protein (check the Structures tab) 3. Try refreshing the page

docs.title: Buttons don't do anything
docs.text:
Cause: Click handlers aren't firing, or the action depends on missing state.

Fix: 1. Check DevTools console for JavaScript errors 2. Make sure you have a sequence loaded (some buttons require one) 3. Make sure you have a folded structure (some buttons require it)

docs.title: Imported sequence doesn't show in textarea
docs.text: Fixed in v0.2.1. Update to the latest version. Old behavior: import would set state but the sidebar textarea had its own local state. New behavior: textarea is bound to the shared currentSequence state.

docs.title: Academy match game doesn't accept correct matches
docs.text: Fixed in v0.2.1. The shuffled list was being re-randomized on every render, causing the visible buttons to change between clicks. Now memoized once.

docs.title: pip install fails on Python 3.14
docs.text:
Cause: Some packages don't yet have wheels for Python 3.14 (it's very new).

Fix: - Use Python 3.11 or 3.12 instead - Or wait for upstream packages to ship 3.14 wheels - Or build from source (requires C compiler)

docs.title: npm install fails with permissions error
docs.text:
Fix: 

# Don't use sudo with npm
npm config set prefix ~/.local
export PATH="$HOME/.local/bin:$PATH"

docs.title: Cargo won't build on Windows
docs.text:
Cause: Missing Visual Studio Build Tools.

Fix: Install Visual Studio Build Tools with the "Desktop development with C++" workload. Then try cargo build again.

docs.title: "PYO3_USE_ABI3_FORWARD_COMPATIBILITY" warning
docs.text:
Cause: Your Python is newer than PyO3's tested versions.

Fix: 

PYO3_USE_ABI3_FORWARD_COMPATIBILITY=1 cargo build

Or set it permanently: - Linux/Mac: Add to your shell rc file - Windows: setx PYO3_USE_ABI3_FORWARD_COMPATIBILITY 1

docs.title: "PermissionError: temp file ... in use" on Windows
docs.text:
Cause: SQLAlchemy holds the SQLite file open after tests finish, Windows doesn't release it before the temp dir cleanup.

Fix: This is cosmetic, tests still pass. Or use the ignore_cleanup_errors=True parameter (already in test_storage.py).

docs.title: Tests fail with "no module named opendna_native"
docs.text:
Cause: Rust bindings aren't built yet.

Fix: 

cargo build  # builds the bindings
# Or use maturin to install them as a Python module

The Python tests don't actually need the Rust bindings, only direct imports of Python modules. So you can ignore this if your tests are passing.

docs.title: Everything is slow
docs.text:
Likely cause: CPU-only mode with a large protein.

Fix: - Use shorter sequences for testing - Get a GPU - Use CUDA torch if you have NVIDIA

docs.title: Browser becomes unresponsive
docs.text:
Cause: Loading a very complex structure into Molstar, or memory leak.

Fix: - Refresh the page (you'll lose unsaved state) - Close the structures you don't need - Use a smaller protein

docs.title: API server uses 100% of one CPU core
docs.text: That's expected during inference. Python's GIL means inference uses one core fully. If you have multiple cores, the rest are still available for other tasks.

docs.title: Disk fills up after a few weeks
docs.text:
Cause: HuggingFace cache and model cache grow.

Fix: 

# Clear HF cache (re-downloads on next use)
rm -rf ~/.cache/huggingface/

# Clear OpenDNA data (loses projects!)
rm -rf ~/.opendna/

docs.title: UniProt fetch fails
docs.text:
Cause: UniProt's API is slow or down.

Fix: Try again in a minute. Status: https://www.uniprot.org/

docs.title: PDB fetch fails
docs.text:
Cause: RCSB PDB server is slow or the ID doesn't exist.

Fix: Verify the PDB ID at https://www.rcsb.org/

docs.title: HuggingFace download fails
docs.text:
Cause: Hub rate limiting or network issue.

Fix: - Try again later - Set an HF_TOKEN: export HF_TOKEN=your_token - Get a token from https://huggingface.co/settings/tokens

docs.title: "I changed the code but the API still uses the old behavior"
docs.text:
The API server doesn't auto-reload by default. Restart it after code changes: 

# Ctrl+C the server, then re-run:
python -c "from opendna.api.server import start_server; start_server(port=8765)"

For dev with auto-reload: 

uvicorn opendna.api.server:app --reload --port 8765

docs.title: "My UI changes don't appear"
docs.text: Vite hot-reloads automatically. If they don't appear: - Check the Vite terminal for build errors - Hard refresh the browser (Ctrl+Shift+R) - Make sure you saved the file

docs.title: "I can't find my saved project"
docs.text: Projects live at ~/.opendna/projects/<name>/workspace.json. The ~ is your home directory: - Linux: /home/yourname/.opendna/ - Mac: /Users/yourname/.opendna/ - Windows: C:\Users\yourname\.opendna\

docs.title: "I deleted .opendna by mistake"
docs.text: Sorry, no recovery. Always back up ~/.opendna/projects/ if you care about them.

docs.title: When All Else Fails
docs.text:
1. Restart everything — API server, UI dev server, browser
  2. Update to latest — git pull and pip install -e ".[dev]"
  3. Check the issues — https://github.com/dyber-pqc/OpenDNA/issues
  4. Open a new issue with:
  5. OS, Python version, Node version
  6. Steps to reproduce
  7. Full error message
  8. Output of python -m pytest tests/python/
  9. Output of curl http://localhost:8765/health

docs.title: OpenDNA Tutorials
docs.text: Step-by-step walkthroughs for common protein engineering tasks.

docs.title: Tutorial 1: Analyze Your First Protein (5 minutes)
docs.text: Goal: Learn the basic OpenDNA workflow on a real protein.

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
1. Start the platform (if not already running):
  2. Terminal 1: python -c "from opendna.api.server import start_server; start_server(port=8765)"
  3. Terminal 2: cd ui && npm run dev

  4. Browser: http://localhost:5173

  5. Import ubiquitin from the famous proteins list:

  6. Click the Import tab in the sidebar
  7. Click ubiquitin in the famous proteins grid
  8. You should see a green toast: "UBC_HUMAN — Loaded 685 aa from Homo sapiens"

  9. The sidebar switches to the Tools tab automatically

  10. Score the sequence instantly (no model download needed):

  11. Click Score Protein

  12. A score card appears showing the overall quality and breakdown

  13. Run the full analysis suite:

  14. Click Full Analysis Suite
  15. Browse through ~12 sections of analysis

  16. Look for: disorder prediction (UBC has some disorder), aggregation risk, phosphorylation sites

  17. Try a famous mutation:

  18. In the Mutate Active Protein field, type K48R
  19. Click Apply Mutation & Refold
  20. This is the famous K48R mutation that disrupts polyubiquitin chain formation

docs.title: What you learned
docs.text:
• How to import proteins from UniProt
  • The instant scoring system
  • The comprehensive analysis suite
  • How to apply point mutations

docs.title: Tutorial 2: Predict and Visualize a 3D Structure (10 minutes)
docs.text: Goal: Fold a small protein and explore the 3D viewer.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text: First-time only: ESMFold model will download (~8 GB). Make sure you have a fast internet connection and ~10 GB free disk space.

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
1. Use a small test sequence:
  2. Paste this in the Tools tab: 
    MKTVRQERLKSIVRILERSKEPVSGAQLAEELS

  3. That's 33 residues, perfect for a quick fold

  4. Click Predict Structure

  5. First time: ESMFold downloads (~8 GB, watch the API terminal)
  6. Subsequent runs: instant model load
  7. For 33 residues on CPU: ~1-2 minutes

  8. For 33 residues on GPU: ~5 seconds

  9. Explore the 3D structure:

  10. Drag the protein to rotate
  11. Scroll to zoom

  12. Right-click drag to pan

  13. Switch to confidence coloring:

  14. Click pLDDT colors at the bottom-left
  15. Blue regions are high confidence

  16. Orange/red regions are low confidence (often loops or disordered)

  17. Save the structure:

  18. Click Save PDB in the header
  19. The PDB file downloads to your computer
  20. You can open it in any other molecular viewer (PyMOL, ChimeraX, etc.)

docs.title: What you learned
docs.text:
• How to fold proteins with ESMFold
  • 3D viewer controls
  • Confidence visualization
  • Exporting PDB files

docs.title: Tutorial 3: Design Alternative Sequences with ESM-IF1 (15 minutes)
docs.text: Goal: Take a folded protein and generate alternative sequences that should fold into the same shape.

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
1. First, fold a protein (use Tutorial 2's sequence or any other)

  2. Click "Design 10 Sequences (ESM-IF1)"

  3. First time: ESM-IF1 model downloads (~600 MB)
  4. Generates 10 alternative sequences

  5. Each is scored by recovery (% of residues matching the original)

  6. Review the results:

  7. A panel appears with the 10 candidates ranked by score
  8. Notice the recovery percentages (typically 4-30% with default temperature)
  9. High recovery = conservative redesign

  10. Low recovery = aggressive redesign of the same fold

  11. Verify a candidate by folding it:

  12. Click Fold on candidate #1
  13. This folds the new sequence

  14. Add it to your structures list

  15. Compare original vs designed:

  16. Switch to the Structures tab
  17. Click vs on the original structure
  18. The viewer splits to show both side-by-side
  19. Visually compare — they should have similar topology if ESM-IF1 worked

docs.title: What you learned
docs.text:
• How inverse folding works
  • Sequence recovery interpretation
  • How to verify designed sequences by re-folding
  • Side-by-side structure comparison

docs.title: Tutorial 4: Iterative Design Loop (30 minutes)
docs.text: Goal: Use OpenDNA's killer feature — automated protein optimization over multiple rounds.

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
1. Pick a starting sequence: 

    MKTVRQERLKSIVRILER
    (18 residues — small enough to iterate quickly on CPU)

  2. Configure iterative design:

  3. In the Iterative Design Loop section
  4. Set rounds: 3 and candidates: 5

  5. That's 15 total designs across 3 rounds

  6. Click "Run Iterative Design"

  7. You'll see a series of toasts as each round runs
  8. Rounds typically take 1-3 minutes each on CPU

  9. Total: ~10 minutes

  10. Review the results panel:

  11. Initial score → Final score with improvement %
  12. Optimization history chart
  13. Best sequence per round

  14. The final structure is auto-added to your structures list

  15. Compare initial and final:

  16. Initial sequence is in your history
  17. Final sequence is the new active structure
  18. Use the vs button to compare them in 3D

docs.title: What you learned
docs.text:
• The iterative optimization concept
  • How to read the score history
  • How to interpret improvement
  • The fold ↔ design feedback loop

docs.title: Try this variation
docs.text: Set rounds to 5, candidates to 10. Watch a longer optimization. See if it converges or keeps improving.

docs.title: Tutorial 5: Famous Mutations to Try
docs.text: These are real mutations from biology and disease. Try them in OpenDNA.

docs.title: Tumor suppressors and oncogenes
docs.text:
KRAS G12D (most common cancer mutation): 1. Import → kras 2. Mutate → G12D 3. This is the famous G12D KRAS mutation found in pancreatic cancer

p53 R175H (tumor suppressor mutation): 1. Import → p53 2. Mutate → R175H 3. This is one of the most common p53 mutations in human cancer

docs.title: Insulin variants
docs.text: Insulin lispro (Humalog) — switch the position of B28 and B29 to make a fast-acting insulin - Import insulin → mutate K28P then P29K (sequentially)

docs.title: Hemoglobin S (sickle cell)
docs.text:
• Import hemoglobin_alpha → not the right chain
  • Try fetching P68871 (hemoglobin beta) → mutate E6V

docs.title: CFTR ΔF508 (cystic fibrosis)
docs.text:
• Fetch P13569 (CFTR)
  • Note: very large protein, don't fold on CPU

docs.title: Tutorial 6: Use the Command Palette (3 minutes)
docs.text: Goal: Master the keyboard-driven workflow.

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
1. Open the palette anywhere: Press Ctrl+K (or Cmd+K on Mac)

  2. Try fuzzy search:

  3. Type "ubiq" → finds "Import ubiquitin"
  4. Type "fold" → finds the fold action

  5. Type "score" → finds the score action

  6. Quick imports:

  7. Ctrl+K → "import gfp" → Enter

  8. The sequence loads automatically

  9. Quick analysis:

  10. Ctrl+K → "analyze" → Enter

  11. Skips clicking

  12. Navigate without a mouse:

  13. Ctrl+K → up/down arrows → Enter
  14. Esc to close

docs.title: Power user tip
docs.text: With single-letter shortcuts (only when not focused on an input): - F = fold current sequence - S = score - A = analyze - Esc = close everything

docs.title: Tutorial 7: The Protein Academy (15 minutes)
docs.text: Goal: Learn protein basics through interactive games.

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
1. Click Academy in the header

  2. Level 1: Amino Acid Match (5 min, +50 XP):

  3. Click letters and names to match them up
  4. 8 random amino acids per game
  5. +10 score per match

  6. Complete to earn 50 XP

  7. Level 2: Memory Quiz (3 min, +75 XP):

  8. 5 multiple-choice questions about amino acid properties

  9. Test your knowledge of charge, structure, function

  10. Level 3: Sequence Reader (5 min, +100 XP):

  11. Tutorial on reading protein sequences
  12. Learn the meaning of each letter

  13. Pattern recognition tips

  14. Watch your XP grow in the header badge

docs.title: Educational notes
docs.text:
• The 20 standard amino acids are the building blocks of all proteins
  • Their letter codes (A, C, D, E, F, G, H, I, K, L, M, N, P, Q, R, S, T, V, W, Y) are universally used
  • Each has different properties: charge, size, hydrophobicity, special function
  • Reading a sequence and predicting properties is the foundational skill

docs.title: Tutorial 8: Compare Two Structures (10 minutes)
docs.text: Goal: Use OpenDNA to compare a wild-type protein and a mutant.

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
1. Fold the wild-type:
  2. Paste: MKTVRQERLKSIVRILER
  3. Click Predict Structure

  4. Wait for the fold

  5. Apply a mutation:

  6. Mutate field: K2D
  7. Click Apply Mutation & Refold

  8. Wait for the new fold

  9. Compare them:

  10. Click the Structures tab
  11. You should see 2 structures
  12. Click vs on the first one (the wild-type)

  13. The viewer splits into two

  14. Visually inspect:

  15. Are they similar topology?
  16. Did the mutation cause any visible changes?

  17. Use both viewers' rotation independently

  18. Quantitative comparison (via API): 

    curl -X POST http://localhost:8765/v1/compare \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"pdb_a": "...", "pdb_b": "..."}'
    Returns RMSD, secondary structure identity, radius of gyration.

docs.title: Tutorial 9: Estimate the Cost of Your Protein
docs.text: Goal: Find out how much it would cost to actually synthesize and produce your designed protein.

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
1. Have a sequence loaded (any size)

  2. Click Cost & Carbon Estimate

  3. Review:

  4. Synthesis costs from Twist Bioscience, IDT, GenScript
  5. Cheapest vendor highlighted

  6. Carbon footprint comparison: CPU vs GPU compute

  7. Use this to plan budget for wet-lab experiments

docs.title: What the estimates mean
docs.text:
• Synthesis = ordering the DNA gene that codes for your protein
  • After ordering: clone into vector → transform into E. coli → grow → purify → assay
  • Total wet-lab cost typically 5-20x the synthesis cost

docs.title: Tutorial 10: Save and Reload a Project
docs.text: Goal: Don't lose your work between sessions.

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
1. Build up some structures (fold a few proteins)

  2. Save:

  3. Press Ctrl+Shift+S (or use Cmd+K → "save project")
  4. Enter a project name (e.g. my_first_project)

  5. Toast confirms save

  6. Find your saved project:

  7. Open the Dashboard (header button)

  8. "Saved Projects" section lists all your projects

  9. Load a project (manually via API for now): 

    curl -X POST http://localhost:8765/v1/projects/load \
  -d '{"name":"my_first_project"}'

  10. UI auto-load is coming in v0.3

docs.title: Where projects live
docs.text: ~/.opendna/projects/<name>/workspace.json

docs.title: Tutorial Cheat Sheet
docs.text: Goal Click path Fold a protein Tools → paste seq → Predict Structure Score instantly Tools → paste seq → Score Protein Full analysis Tools → Full Analysis Suite Apply mutation Tools → Mutate field → Apply Design alternatives Tools → Design 10 Sequences Iterative optimization Tools → Run Iterative Design Import famous protein Import → click name (or Cmd+K → "import X") Compare two structures Structures tab → vs button AI explanation Tools → Explain This Protein Cost estimate Tools → Cost & Carbon Estimate Quick MD Tools → Quick MD Open Dashboard Header → Dashboard Open Academy Header → Academy Command palette Ctrl+K Save project Ctrl+Shift+S Save PDB Ctrl+S or header button

docs.title: OpenDNA User Guide
docs.text: The complete reference for using OpenDNA. Every feature, every button, every panel.

docs.title: Table of Contents
docs.text:
1. Interface Overview
  2. The Sidebar
  3. The 3D Viewer
  4. The Chat Panel
  5. The Header Bar
  6. The Command Palette
  7. Notifications (Toasts)
  8. Analysis Panel
  9. Dashboard
  10. Protein Academy
  11. Iterative Design
  12. Keyboard Shortcuts

docs.title: The Sidebar
docs.text: The sidebar has 3 tabs:

docs.title: Tools Tab
docs.text: The main workspace. Contains:

docs.title: Protein Sequence
docs.text:
• Textarea — Paste any amino acid sequence here. Updates in real-time as you import or edit.
  • Length indicator — Shows residue count once a sequence is loaded.
  • Predict Structure button — Submits to ESMFold (folds the protein in 3D).
  • Score Protein button — Instant sequence-only quality score.

docs.title: Iterative Design Loop
docs.text:
• Rounds × Candidates controls — Set how many optimization rounds and how many candidates per round.
  • Run Iterative Design button — Automated optimization loop.

docs.title: Mutate Active Protein
docs.text:
• Mutation input — Format like K48R (from-position-to). 1-indexed.
  • Apply Mutation & Refold — Applies the mutation and immediately refolds the new sequence.

docs.title: Active Structure Tools
docs.text: Available actions on the currently-loaded protein: - Design 10 Sequences (ESM-IF1) — Generate 10 alternative sequences for the active backbone. - Full Analysis Suite — Open the comprehensive analysis panel. - Explain This Protein (AI) — Get a plain-English explanation (uses Ollama if available). - Quick MD (stability check) — Run a fast molecular dynamics check. - Cost & Carbon Estimate — Show synthesis $$ and CO₂ estimates.

docs.title: Structures Tab
docs.text: History of all folded structures in the current session. - Click any structure to make it active in the viewer. - Click the vs button to compare it side-by-side with the active structure. - Active structure has a highlighted border. - Each shows: name preview, length, mean pLDDT confidence.

docs.title: Import Tab
docs.text: Load proteins from external databases. - Source toggle — UniProt or PDB - ID input — Enter accession (e.g. P0CG48) or famous name (e.g. ubiquitin) - Fetch button — Loads the protein and switches to the Tools tab - Famous proteins grid — Quick-access for ubiquitin, insulin, GFP, lysozyme, myoglobin, p53, KRAS, EGFR

docs.title: The 3D Viewer
docs.text: The main visualization area, powered by Molstar.

docs.title: Mouse Controls
docs.text:
• Drag — Rotate
  • Scroll — Zoom
  • Right-click drag — Translate
  • Click an atom — Show info popup

docs.title: Color Modes (bottom-left buttons)
docs.text:
• pLDDT colors — AlphaFold-style confidence coloring (blue=high, red=low)
  • Chain colors — Color by chain ID (default Molstar)

docs.title: Stats Overlay (top-right)
docs.text: Shows total atoms and residues in the loaded structure.

docs.title: Side-by-Side Compare
docs.text: When you click "vs" on a structure in the Structures tab, the viewer splits in half showing both structures simultaneously. Each viewer can be controlled independently.

docs.title: The Chat Panel
docs.text: A natural-language interface at the bottom of the main area.

docs.title: What you can say
docs.text:
• fold MKTVRQERLKSIVRILER — Predicts structure for any sequence
  • score MKTVRQERLK — Evaluates a sequence
  • mutate K48R — Applies a mutation to the active protein
  • explain — Gets an AI explanation of the active protein
  • help — Lists available commands

docs.title: Behind the scenes
docs.text:
The chat panel sends your message to /v1/chat which: 1. Tries Ollama (llama3.2:3b) if running 2. Falls back to a deterministic regex-based intent parser 3. Maps the intent to UI actions

If you install Ollama and run ollama pull llama3.2:3b, you get richer natural language understanding for free.

docs.title: Left side
docs.text:
• Logo — OpenDNA brand
  • Version — Current build version (e.g. v0.2.0-beta)
  • XP badge — Your accumulated experience points from Academy

docs.title: Right side
docs.text:
• ⌘K button — Opens the command palette (or press Ctrl+K)
  • Dashboard button — Opens the Dashboard overlay
  • Academy button — Opens the Protein Academy
  • Save PDB — Downloads the active structure (only shown when one is loaded)
  • ☀/☾ — Toggle dark/light theme

docs.title: The Command Palette
docs.text:
Press Ctrl+K (or Cmd+K on Mac) anywhere in the app.

A modal appears with a search box and list of all available commands grouped by category:

  • Action — fold, score, analyze, explain, design, iterative, MD, cost
  • File — save PDB, save project
  • Import — quick load famous proteins (ubiquitin, insulin, GFP, lysozyme, p53, KRAS, etc.)
  • View — open Dashboard, Academy, toggle theme

docs.title: Navigation
docs.text:
• Type to filter (fuzzy match)
  • ↑/↓ to navigate
  • Enter to execute the highlighted command
  • Esc to close

docs.title: Notifications (Toasts)
docs.text:
Toast notifications appear in the top-right whenever: - A job starts, completes, or fails - An action succeeds (sequence loaded, mutation applied, etc.) - An error occurs

Each toast has: - An icon (✓ success, ✕ error, ⓘ info, ⚠ warning) - A title (sometimes) - A message - A close button (×)

Toasts auto-dismiss after 5 seconds.

docs.title: Analysis Panel
docs.text: The Full Analysis Suite opens a comprehensive analysis overlay with these sections:

docs.title: 1. Sequence Properties
docs.text: QikProp-equivalent table: - Length, MW, isoelectric point (pI) - GRAVY hydropathy - Aromaticity, aliphatic index - Charge at pH 7 - Instability index (with stable/unstable classification) - Extinction coefficient (reduced and oxidized cysteines) - N-terminal half-life (mammalian reticulocytes)

docs.title: 2. Lipinski's Rule of Five
docs.text:
Drug-likeness check: - MW ≤ 500 - H-bond donors ≤ 5 - H-bond acceptors ≤ 10 - LogP ≤ 5

Pass/fail badge with violation list.

docs.title: 3. Hydropathy Profile
docs.text: SVG line chart showing Kyte-Doolittle hydropathy across the sequence with a 9-residue window. Peaks above 1.6 over ~19 residues suggest transmembrane regions.

docs.title: 4. Intrinsic Disorder
docs.text: Per-residue disorder scores with identified disordered regions. p53 famously shows ~70% disorder.

docs.title: 5. Amino Acid Composition
docs.text: Bar chart of all 20 amino acid percentages.

docs.title: 6. Transmembrane Prediction
docs.text: TMHMM-like prediction of membrane-spanning helices.

docs.title: 7. Signal Peptide
docs.text: SignalP-like detection of N-terminal signal peptides for secreted proteins. Shows score and predicted cleavage site.

docs.title: 8. Aggregation Risk
docs.text: TANGO-like prediction of aggregation-prone regions. Color-coded risk level (low/medium/high).

docs.title: 9. Post-Translational Modifications
docs.text:
• Phosphorylation sites by kinase consensus motif (PKA, PKC, CK2, CDK, GSK3, MAPK)
  • N-glycosylation sites (NXS/T sequon)
  • O-glycosylation sites (mucin-like regions)

docs.title: 10. Secondary Structure (if structure is loaded)
docs.text:
• Helix / strand / coil percentages
  • Visual ribbon showing per-residue assignment
  • Radius of gyration
  • SASA estimate

docs.title: 11. Ramachandran Plot
docs.text: Phi/psi scatter for all residues. Background regions show typical alpha-helix (blue) and beta-sheet (orange) zones.

docs.title: 12. Binding Pockets
docs.text: Heuristic pocket detection ranked by score.

docs.title: 13. Bond Network (if structure loaded)
docs.text:
• H-bond count
  • Salt bridge count
  • Disulfide bond count with paired cysteines

docs.title: Dashboard
docs.text:
Click Dashboard in the header (or Cmd+K → Dashboard).

Shows: - Stat cards: structures in session, completed jobs, running jobs, failed jobs, saved projects - Hardware table: CPU, cores, RAM, GPU, backend, tier - Recent jobs table: ID, type, status, progress for the last 10 jobs - Saved projects table: name, structure count, save date

docs.title: Protein Academy
docs.text: Click Academy in the header.

docs.title: Available Levels
docs.text:
• Level 1: Amino Acid Match (5 min, +50 XP)
  • Drag-drop matching game for 8 random amino acids
  • Match the 1-letter code to the full name
  • Level 2: Memory Quiz (3 min, +75 XP)
  • 5 multiple-choice questions about amino acid properties
  • Level 3: Sequence Reader (5 min, +100 XP)
  • Tutorial on reading protein sequences
  • Level 4: Famous Proteins Tour (planned)
  • Level 5: Drug Design 101 (planned)

docs.title: XP and Badges
docs.text: Each completed level awards XP that's displayed in the header badge. Future versions will add badges and a skill tree.

docs.title: Iterative Design
docs.text: The killer feature. Automated protein optimization loop.

docs.title: How it works
docs.text:
1. Round 0: Fold your starting sequence and score it
  2. Round 1: Use ESM-IF1 to generate N candidate alternatives. Score each.
  3. Keep best: If any candidate scored higher, fold it to confirm and keep it as the new best.
  4. Repeat for the configured number of rounds.

docs.title: Settings
docs.text:
• Rounds (1-10): How many iterations to run
  • Candidates per round (1-20): How many alternatives to try each round

docs.title: Output
docs.text: A panel with: - Initial score → Final score with improvement % - Optimization history chart (score per round) - Best sequence per round with view buttons - Final structure auto-added to your structures list

docs.title: Cost
docs.text:
• 3 rounds × 5 candidates = 15 design samples + ~6 fold operations
  • On CPU: ~30 minutes for a 50-residue protein
  • On GPU: ~2 minutes

docs.title: Keyboard Shortcuts
docs.text:
Shortcut Action Ctrl+K / Cmd+K Open command palette Ctrl+/ Open command palette F Fold current sequence S Score current sequence A Run analysis suite Esc Close any open overlay Ctrl+S Save current PDB Ctrl+Shift+S Save project workspace

Shortcuts that don't conflict with text inputs work everywhere. Single-letter shortcuts only work when not focused on an input.

docs.title: Speed up CPU folding
docs.text:
• Use shorter sequences first to test (under 50 aa folds in seconds)
  • The result cache means folding the same sequence twice is instant
  • If you have NVIDIA GPU, install CUDA torch for ~50x speedup

docs.title: Get richer AI explanations
docs.text:
Install Ollama and pull a small model: 

ollama pull llama3.2:3b
ollama serve  # in background
Then "Explain This Protein" uses real LLM responses instead of the heuristic fallback.

docs.title: Analyze multiple proteins
docs.text: Use the Structures tab to keep a history. The compare button lets you put two side-by-side.

docs.title: Find binding pockets
docs.text: Run Full Analysis Suite on a folded protein. The "Putative Binding Pockets" section ranks the top 5 cavities.

docs.title: Understand your protein in plain English
docs.text: Use Explain This Protein (AI) for a friendly summary that even a high school student can understand.
